metadata.title: Why a Perfect-Looking Spreadsheet Can Be Off by a Factor of Ten

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Jambo, Liam — in for Bear. And row twenty-three is off by a factor of ten? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: And row twenty-three is off by a factor of ten?
props.segment: Why a Perfect-Looking Spreadsheet Can Be

beats.narration_text: Forty-seven receipts. Three minutes. A clean, forty-seven-row spreadsheet. And row twenty-three is off by a factor of ten.

card.copy: Why a Perfect-Looking Spreadsheet Can Be Off by a Factor of Ten

beats.narration_text: Nothing on the surface announces this. The headers are right, the columns line up, the decimal places are uniform. The formatting is professional.

props.headline: Nothing on the surface announces this. The headers are right, the…

beats.scene_description: a clean professional spreadsheet on a desk — column headers neatly labeled, every cell filled, perfectly formatted, slow push-in on the upper-left corner
beats.image_prompt: B02, top-down view of a clean printed spreadsheet on a desk, evenly spaced columns with bold headers, all cells filled with numbers and text, professional and orderly, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, no visible errors, cream and charcoal tones

beats.narration_text: Here is the question. A pristine spreadsheet should mean correct data. This one is spotless — and row twenty-three is wrong by ten times. Why?

document.quote: A pristine spreadsheet should mean correct data. This one is spotless and row 23 is wrong by ten times. Why?

beats.narration_text: Here are two cells. Both formatted identically. One reads three-forty-two. The source says thirty-four-twenty. The formatting is indistinguishable. The values are not.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two cells same format, different values

beats.narration_text: Because extraction has two separate steps. First: the tool reads the document. Second: it writes the table. That second step always works. Always.

viz.label: READ -> FORMAT pipeline, FORMAT always ticks TEAL

beats.narration_text: The formatting step cannot see whether the reading succeeded. A blurry photo, a shifted decimal — the tool reads it, writes a clean cell, moves on.

viz.label: READ with CRIMSON X -> FORMAT with TEAL check -> clean cell output

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] read fails, format still produces clean cell

beats.narration_text: When the reading step guesses, it writes that guess with identical authority. Unknown Merchant — one smudged receipt, silently copied into three more rows. No flag.

viz.label: 4 rows Unknown Merchant in CRIMSON, GOLD sweep fill across all four

beats.narration_text: This is the mechanism: structure confers credibility the numbers have not earned. Formatting always succeeds. Reading sometimes fails. The output shows only one of those.

document.quote: Structure confers credibility the numbers have not earned.

beats.narration_text: Trusting a spreadsheet because it looks official means inferring something the format cannot tell you. Format is evidence that formatting ran. Nothing more.

props.headline: Trusting a spreadsheet because it looks official means inferring something the…

beats.scene_description: a person at a desk confidently marking a cell in a printed spreadsheet with a red pen, ready to pass it on — trusting format as accuracy
beats.image_prompt: B10, close-up of hands at a desk holding a red pen and marking a cell in a clean printed spreadsheet, confident reviewing posture, document looks official, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, warm cream tones, no face visible

beats.narration_text: Here is how it plays out — illustrative numbers, real mechanism. Forty-seven receipts extracted. Row twenty-three reads three-forty-two. The source says thirty-four-twenty. The decimal point moved.

viz.label: mini spreadsheet rows, row 23 CRIMSON $342.00, TEAL arrow to source $34.20, 'illustrative' label
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE act — label illustrative per FACTCHECK

beats.narration_text: Three minutes to extract, ten to spot-check five rows. Two errors found. Forty-two rows unchecked. The table looks the same either way.

viz.label: 47 row-marks: 5 TEAL-ticked, 2 CRIMSON error, 42 INK neutral

beats.narration_text: Clean format means the table ran. It does not mean the reading was right. Verify the numbers — the format will not.

card.copy: Clean format does not mean correct data.

beats.new_visual_element: [ENDCARD] clean format does not mean correct data

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. Three minutes to extract, ten to spot-check five rows. Two errors found. Forty-two rows unchecked. The table looks the same either way. Clean format means the table ran. It does not mean the reading was right. Verify the numbers — the format will not.

artifactLines: Here is how it plays out — illustrative numbers, real mechanism.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why a Perfect-Looking Spreadsheet Can Be Off by a Factor of Ten] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
